coredata.dc:title: Interfacial enhancement effect of graphene quantum dots on PEDOT:PSS/single-walled carbon nanotubes thermoelectric materials
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Ping
Xiao, Jin-Kun
Gong, Jia-Zhi
Zhu, Ying
Yao, Jun-An
Zhang, Yun-Fei
Wang, Sheng-Gao
Lin, Zhi-Dong
Du, Fei-Peng
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonic acid) (PEDOT:PSS), with the incorporation of graphene quantum dot (GQD)-coated single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), has been used in the preparation of PEDOT:PSS/SWCNTs/GQDs thermoelectric films (PSG). The effect of SWCNTs/GQDs (as a nanofiller) on the microstructure and thermoelectric properties of the composite films has been especially investigated. It was observed that the addition of GQDs will induce a phase separation between PEDOT and PSS. Furthermore, the enhanced interfacial bonding induced a microstructure ordering and an accelerated transport of carriers. The in-plane electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient of the obtained film reached 1036 S•cm−1 and 13.8 μV•K−1, respectively, for 1 wt% of SWCNTs/GQDs. These results gave a high power factor (PF) of up to 19.9 µW•m−1•K−2, which is about six times as high as for pristine PEDOT:PSS and also higher than that of PEDOT:PSS/SWCNTs. The obtained maximum ZT reaches 3.22 × 10−3 with high PF value and low in-plane thermal conductivity. The present research provided a new strategy for developing high performance organic thermoelectric materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEDOT:PSS
Graphene quantum dots
Single-walled carbon nanotubes
Hybrid
Thermoelectric properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) GQDs, (b) SWCNTs, and (c) SG.
Cross-section SEM images: (a) PEDOT:PSS, (b) PSG1, (c) PSG5, and (d) PSG10.
A schematic showing interfacial interaction between SWCNTs@GQDs and PEDOT:PSS during the preparing process.
Raman spectra of PEDOT:PSS, PS and PSG.
Hall mobility and charge carrier concentration of the PSG composite films.
In-plane thermoelectric properties of PS and PSG composite films at room temperature.
In-plane and through-plane parameter of thermoelectric properties (T = 25 °C).

head.ce:title: Interfacial enhancement effect of graphene quantum dots on PEDOT:PSS/single-walled carbon nanotubes thermoelectric materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, 430205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonic acid) (PEDOT:PSS), with the incorporation of graphene quantum dot (GQD)-coated single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), has been used in the preparation of PEDOT:PSS/SWCNTs/GQDs thermoelectric films (PSG). The effect of SWCNTs/GQDs (as a nanofiller) on the microstructure and thermoelectric properties of the composite films has been especially investigated. It was observed that the addition of GQDs will induce a phase separation between PEDOT and PSS. Furthermore, the enhanced interfacial bonding induced a microstructure ordering and an accelerated transport of carriers. The in-plane electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient of the obtained film reached 1036 S•cm−1 and 13.8 μV•K−1, respectively, for 1 wt% of SWCNTs/GQDs. These results gave a high power factor (PF) of up to 19.9 µW•m−1•K−2, which is about six times as high as for pristine PEDOT:PSS and also higher than that of PEDOT:PSS/SWCNTs. The obtained maximum ZT reaches 3.22 × 10−3 with high PF value and low in-plane thermal conductivity. The present research provided a new strategy for developing high performance organic thermoelectric materials.

ce:section.ce:para:
Organic thermoelectric materials (OTM) have recently received an increased attention for their use in converting waste heat to electric energy [1–3]. The energy conversion efficiency of thermoelectric materials is defined by the dimensionless figure-of-merit
Z T =
S
2
σ T / κ
, where S is the Seebeck coefficient, σ is the electrical conductivity, T is the absolute temperature and κ is the thermal conductivity. Compared to inorganic thermoelectric materials, OTM have demonstrated an obvious advantage as a low cost and light weight material with excellent flexibility and low thermal conductivity [4–7]. Among the most common OTM, poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonic acid) (PEDOT:PSS) shows excellent water solubility and film-forming ability and is thereby considered as an important organic material in the thermoelectric field [8–10]. However, PEDOT:PSS has, without any treatment, the low power factor
P F =
S
2
σ
[11]. There are three different ways to improve the thermoelectric performances, and those are to use (i) solvent secondary doping, (ii) solvent post-treatment, and (iii) nanofillers. Secondary doping and post-treatment both can tune the electron state and molecular chain structure of PEDOT:PSS [12–18]. Secondary doping can induce a detachment of the PSS chains from the PEDOT chains, which results in an increase of bi-polaron percentage and the number of delocalized electrons in the PEDOT chains [11]. Chao et al. prepared a PEDOT:PSS film that was doped by using binary solvents including 0.3% PEO and 5% DMSO [14]. The obtained electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, and PF were 300 S•cm−1, 40 μV•K−1, and 157.35 µW•m−1•K−2, respectively. In fact, the PF was 10,000 times higher than that of a film with no secondary doping [14]. A post-treatment using solvents can remove the PSS from the surface and interior of the PEDOT:PSS films. In addition, it can also change the PEDOT:PSS structure, from a curled coil structure to an expanded coil structure [15–18]. Luo et al. prepared PEDOT:PSS films by using a DMSO post-treatment. The resulting Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, and PF were 17.99 μV•K−1, 930.41 S•cm−1, and 30.1 µW•m−1•K−2, respectively [16]. Furthermore, Liu et al. investigated the influence of different polar solvents on the thermoelectric properties of PEDOT:PSS films. They showed that an EG-treated PEDOT:PSS film can exhibit a maximum PF of 14.9 µW•m−1•K−2, with an electrical conductivity of 520 S•cm−1 and a Seebeck coefficient of 17 μV•K−1 [17]. Pipe et al. combined secondary doping with post-treatment in preparing PEDOT:PSS thermoelectric films. These films showed a high thermoelectric performance with a PF of ~469 µW•m−1•K−2 and a maximum ZT value of 0.42 (by the addition of 5 vol% DMSO and by using an EG post-treatment) [18]. Besides, acids, alkalis, and salts do all show strong interactions with the PEDOT:PSS structure, and their incorporation can change the coil structure to a more ordered one [19–25].

The use of nanofillers in the polymer thermoelectric matrix can induce energy-filtering effect and inhibit low energy carriers transforming at the interfaces to increase the Seebeck coefficient [26–30]. Carbon nanotubes, as one-dimensional (1D) structure, demonstrate a high electrical conductivity and large specific surface area (SSA) [31,32]. The 1D structure, with high electrical conductivity, can form a good conductive path in the polymer matrix [33–35]. In addition, the large SSA leads to effective energy-filtering at the interfaces [36–38]. When combining conductive polymers with carbon nanotubes, the π-π interactions will further promote the carrier transport [39–41]. Kim et al. added 35 wt% of SWCNTs to PEDOT:PSS and obtained a PF of ~25 µW•m−1•K−2 [42]. Yu et al. added single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and polyvinyl acetate (PVAc) to PEDOT:PSS. The resulting nanocomposite film showed a Seebeck coefficient of 27–41 μV•K−1 and an optimal PF of ~160 µW•m−1•K−2 (for 60 wt% SWCNTs) [43]. Moreover, Moriarty et al. prepared PEDOT:PSS/SWCNTs nanocomposites by using a liquid-phase exfoliation method. They obtained the highest PF of 140 µW•m−1•K−2 with 85 wt% SWCNTs [44]. However, the weak interfacial bonding between carbon nanotubes and PEDOT: PSS restricts the improvement of thermoelectric performances. Suitable surface modification of the carbon nanotubes can facilitate their dispersion in a polymer matrix and improve the charge transport to enhance the PF [45–47]. Tong et al. modified SWCNTs with carboxylic acid and hydroxyl [46]. The modification of the SWCNTs both increased the electrical conductivity and the Seebeck coefficient of PEDOT:PSS [46]. Chung et al. added acid treated SWCNTs to PEDOT:PSS, which resulted in a larger PF than that of pure PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT:PSS/pristine SWCNTs [47] . Imae et al. mixed 74 wt% of SWCNTs with aqueous solution of PEDOT:PSS, and SWCNTs was modified with the surfactant sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS). A high electric conductivity of 3800 S•cm−1, and a Seebeck coefficient of 28 μV•K−1, were obtained by soaking this mixture with DMSO for 2 min at room temperature. This leads to the promising PF of 300 µW•m−1•K−2, and a ZT value of 0.13 [48].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a zero-dimensional graphene material, contain a lot of hydrophilic groups [49]. In this paper, SWCNTs were coated with GQDs via π-π interactions. The obtained hydrophilic surfaces enable SWCNTs well-dispersed in PEDOT:PSS, and flexible PEDOT:PSS/SWCNTs/GQDs thermoelectric films (PSG) were prepared by using a simple casting method. The effect of SWCNTs solubilization, in combination with an induced phase separation, improved the thermoelectric properties of the PEDOT:PSS films.

PEDOT:PSS (PH1000, 1.0–1.3 wt%) was supplied from Heraeus Clevios™ (Germany). SWCNTs (>85%) were provided by Shenzhen Nanotech Port Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, China). All other chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). They were of analytical grade and used without any further purification.

The GQDs were prepared as described in our previous report [50]. The GQDs were dispersed into deionized water, forming a 1 mg/mL GQDs suspension. A 0.5 mg/mL suspension of SWCNTs was, thereafter, prepared by dispersing SWCNTs into ethanol. Finally, SWCNTs/GQDs hybrids (SG) were formed by mixing SWCNTs and GQDs suspensions, followed by an ultrasonic treatment for 1 h and a final drying (in an oven) at 100 °C. The mass ratio of SWCNTs to GQDs was 1:1.

SG were added into a PEDOT:PSS solution and stirred rigorously at room temperature for 24 h. The PEDOT:PSS/SG (PSG) films were finally obtained by casting the dispersion on the surface of a glass slide (and drying at 50 °C for 24 h). Finally, the obtained PSG films were soaked in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) for 5 min and, thereafter dried at 50 °C for 24 h. The mass ratio of PEDOT:PSS to SG was 99:1, 95:5 and 90:10, respectively, and the corresponding PSG films were denoted as PSG1, PSG5 and PSG10. For comparison, a pure PEDOT:PSS film and PEDOT:PSS/SWCNTs films (denoted as PS1, PS5, and PS10, with a mass ratio of PEDOT:PSS to SWCNTs identical to the one for PEDOT:PSS to SG) were prepared by using the same procedure as for PSG1, PSG5 and PSG10.

The morphologies of the GQDs, SWCNTs and SG were characterized by using transmission electronic microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). The cross-section microstructures of the PSG films were obtained by using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI SU8010, Japan). Furthermore, Raman spectra were obtained by using a DXR Raman microscope (Thermo Scientific, USA) with a 532 nm laser excitation. The electrical conductivity (σ ∥), and Seebeck coefficient along in-plane direction of the films, were analyzed at room temperature by using a thermoelectric parameter test system (Namicro-III, Wuhan Schwab Instruments, China) with a 7.5 mm probe spacing. The temperature gradient for the collection points was set from 0.3° to 3.5°C along the longitudinal direction of the samples. All samples had similar dimensions, with an area of 14.0 mm × 14.0 mm [length (l) × width (w)] and a thickness of approximately 10 µm. Also, average values were obtained from five different samples. The PF was calculated from Eq. (1):
(1)
P F =
S
2
σ

The through-plane electrical conductivity (σ ⊥) was measured via four-probe method along the thickness direction. The mobility and carrier concentration of the samples were measured using the Hall effect measurement system (Pasic-Hall-HT50, Precision Systems Industrial Ltd., China), and the Hall effect measurements were recorded at a constant magnetic field (0.2 T).

The in-plane thermal diffusivity (α ∥) and through-plane thermal diffusivity (α ⊥) of the samples were measured by the laser flash method (LFA467, Netzsch). The testing samples have been tailored to 24.8 mm in diameter and 20 µm in thickness for in-plane thermal diffusivity, and 10 × 10 mm square-shape with 20 µm in thickness for through-plane thermal diffusivity. The in-plane thermal conductivity (κ∥) and through-plane thermal conductivity (κ⊥) were evaluated using the relationship κ = ρ × C p × α, where ρ is the mass density, C p is the specific heat capacity, and α is the thermal diffusivity of the samples [51]. The ρ was calculated via measuring the volume and mass of the film, and C p was obtained by using a differential scanning calorimeter (Mettler DSC3, Mettler Toledo, Switzerland).

As can be seen in Fig. 1, the GQDs has an average diameter of 15 nm, which has also been measured and described in one of our previous publications [51]. The purchased SWCNTs was a mixture of metallic and semiconducting tubes with ca. 1–2 nm of diameter and 5–30 µm of length. SWCNTs were clustered together and the SWCNTs bundles were smooth and uniform. The average diameter of the bundles was approximately 30 nm (Fig. 1b). Some impurities appeared amongst the SWCNTs bundles, which were the residual catalyst during the preparation of the SWCNTs. After hybridizing, GQDs with 5–15 nm of diameter was found to be attached to the surface of SWCNTs bundles (Fig. 1c).

As can be seen in Fig. 2a, uniform grains are present in a cross-section of the PEDOT:PSS matrix. They are adhered to each other, forming a continuous phase. These grains are constructed from PEDOT-rich cores that are surrounded by PSS-rich shells [52]. Furthermore, the cross-section images of the PSG showed layered structures with alternating SWCNTs and PEDOT:PSS layers. The formed layers are parallel to the in-plane direction of the films. The layered structure became more prominent with an increase in hybrid content, and was responsible for the phase separation between PEDOT:PSS and the hybrid. The PEDOT cores experience a weak interaction with the SWCNTs, which is due to the wrapping of the PEDOT by PSS chains [53]. However, when the GQDs were coated on the surface of the SWCNTs, the SWCNTs became well dispersed in the PEDOT:PSS solution. The GQDs did at first interact with the PSS via hydrophilic groups and, secondly, induced a separation of PSS from the PEDOT (as described in a previous work [50]). This process gave the SWCNTs a chance to interact with the bare PEDOT cores. Consequently, the adhesion between the SWCNTs and PEDOT:PSS became strong at the interfaces, which was also apparent in the SEM images. Besides, a high aspect ratio brings a strong entanglement amongst the SWCNTs during the mixing process, which can induce the phase separation between the SWCNTs and the polymer matrix. In fact, a PEDOT:PSS layered structure is easily formed via secondary doping and post-treatment [54]. The removal of the excess of PSS results in an expansion of the PEDOT chain and brings about a strong π-π interaction between the PEDOT chains (and accordingly form an ordered structure). In addition, the SG are observed to plunge into the PEDOT:PSS layers, forming bridges between the PEDOT:PSS layers (which is due to the strong interactions between the SG and PEDOT:PSS) (Fig. 2b and c). It is believed that the multilayered structures will reduce the phonon thermal conductivity, which can be ascribed to a strong interfacial phone scattering [41,55]. This is beneficial for the increase of ZT value. The SWCNTs layered structure is hardly visible at low SG contents but the interpenetration of SG into the matrix is easily observed (Fig. 2b).

On the other hand, a SWCNTs layered structure is obtained at a high SG content, which is due to the enhanced entanglement among the nanotubes (Fig. 2c and d). A schematic illustration of interfacial interaction between SWCNTs@GQDs and PEDOT:PSS is shown in Fig. 3.

As can be seen in the Raman spectra in Fig. 4, PEDOT:PSS has three main peaks including a CC inter-ring stretching (1256 cm−1), a CC single-bond stretching (1364 cm−1), and a CC symmetrical stretching (1429 cm−1) [53]. The peak at 1429 cm−1 is assigned to the Cα = Cβ symmetric vibration for the five-member thiophene ring on the PEDOT chains. The peak at 1429 cm−1 does not move after adding SWCNTs observed from the curve of PS1 and PS5, indicating that the interaction between SWCNTs and PEDOT is very weak. However, the peak at 1429 cm−1 move to high wavenumber after SWCNTs were hybridized with GQDs and the blue-shift occurred. This indicates that GQDs on the surface of SWCNTs enhances the interaction between SWCNTs and PEDOT. The blue-shift was even more pronounced with an increase in SG content.

The incorporation of SG into PEDOT:PSS also affected the carrier parameters ( Fig. 5). The mobility increased, and the carrier concentration decreased, with an increase in SG content. The mobility of PEDOT:PSS was 0.24 cm2/(V•s), while the mobility of PSG10 reached 10.9 cm2/(V•s). The carrier concentration of PEDOT:PSS was 23.9 × 1020/cm3, while it reduced to 0.38 × 1020/cm3 for PSG10. Moreover, the carrier concentration decreased with an increase in SG content, originated from the energy filtering effect. At the interface between the SG and PEDOT:PSS, the barrier hinders the transfer of low-energy carriers and, thereby, reduces the total carrier concentration. The increase in mobility, with an increase in SG content, is attributed to the ordered layered structure, which is induced by the interaction between the SG and PEDOT. On the surface of the hybrid, both the SWCNTs and the GQDs experience π-π interactions with PEDOT. In addition, the PEDOT chains become assembled in an ordered way on the surface, which results in a fast carrier transport.

The in-plane thermoelectric properties of PS and PSG films are shown in Fig. 6. All samples have undergone solvent post-treatments. The σ ∥ of PEDOT:PSS was 307 S•cm−1. Furthermore, the σ ∥ of the PSGs did first increase, and then decreased, with increasing SG content from 1 wt% to 10 wt%. It reached 1036 S•cm−1 for PSG1, which was three times as high as compared with PEDOT:PSS. However, the σ ∥ of PSG5 and PSG10 was 534 S•cm−1 and 334 S•cm−1, respectively. A high SG loading produced strong interfacial carriers scattering in these materials, which contributed to the decrease in electrical conductivity. The PS films, without any GQDs incorporation, showed the same trend in electrical conductivity. However, σ ∥ were slightly higher than those of the PSG films (Fig. 6a). Therefore, a suitable SG loading will induce an ordered microstructure via strong interactions between the SWCNTs and the polymer. This ordering can enhance the carrier transport and improve the electric conductivity (although the strong interfacial interaction may cause strong carrier scattering).

The in-plane Seebeck coefficient of the PSG films increased with an increase in the SG content. The Seebeck coefficient of PEDOT:PSS was 10.5 μV•K−1. Furthermore, the Seebeck coefficients were 13.8 µV•K−1, 14.0 μV•K−1 and 14.5 μV•K−1 for PSG1, PSG5 and PSG10, respectively. The increase of the Seebeck coefficient was attributed to the decrease in carrier concentration due to the energy-filtering effect at the interface [56]. The PS films showed smaller Seebeck coefficients than the series of PSG (Fig. 6b). Therefore, the incorporation of GQDs at the interface helps to improve the Seebeck coefficient due to the energy-filtering effect.

The PF of PEDOT:PSS was 3.4 µW•m−1•K−2. Furthermore, the PF of PSG1 was 19.9 µW•m−1•K−2, which is about six times as high as for PEDOT:PSS. It is also higher than those of the PS series. GQDs were well dispersed in PEDOT:PSS, and the strong interaction between the GQDs and PEDOT:PSS enhanced the Seebeck coefficient and electrical conductivity simultaneously [50]. The PF of PEDOT:PSS/GQDs was increased by 550% (as compared with PEDOT:PSS). In fact, the unique quantum confinement, and the edge effects of the GQDs, will effectively improve the thermoelectric properties of the composites. Li et al. embedded GQDs homogeneously in the Bi2Te3 nanosheet matrix in the synthesis of Bi2Te3/Graphene quantum dot (Bi2Te3/GQDs) hybrid nanosheets [57]. The introduction of GQDs not only introduced quantum-confinement effects in enhancing the thermoelectric performance, but also strengthened the phonon scattering at the charged Bi2Te3/GQDs interfaces (with high-density distorted regions and reduced thermal conductivity). By adjusting the size of the GQDs (to approximately 20 nm), the ZT value of the Bi2Te3/GQDs composite became 1.6 times higher than that of pristine Bi2Te3. Liang et al. used GQDs as a ligand exchanger to prevent PbSe and CdSe nanoparticle overgrowth and aggregation [58]. A high ZT value of 0.46 at 650 K was then obtained [58]. The beneficial thermoelectric properties were a result from the quantum confinement and carrier energy filtering caused by the GQDs. The coating of GQDs on the surfaces of SWCNTs enhanced the interaction between the SWCNTs and PEDOT:PSS, and improved the thermoelectric properties of PSG.

The σ ⊥ of PEDOT:PSS was 100.93 S/cm, increasing with the increase of SG content ( Table 1). Fig. 2 shows SWCNTs stretch across the films along the through-plane direction, which is beneficial to improve the σ ⊥. Comparing the σ ⊥ of PSG and PS, it is found that the σ ⊥ of PS is higher than that of PSG. It may be that the oxygen-containing groups of GQDs inhibits the carriers transforming between PEDOT:PSS and SWCNTs. Comparing the σ ⊥ and σ ∥, it can be found that σ ∥ (Fig. 6a) is much higher than σ ⊥. The ordered layered structure in plane is favorable for carrier transport and contributes a lot to the electrical conductivity. For all the films, the thickness of the film can be no more than 6 mm, and σ ⊥ and κ ⊥ can be measured. However, the Seebeck coefficient can not be measured in the thickness direction in this study. Therefore, it is difficult to investigate the through-plane thermoelectric properties.

The κ ⊥ of PEDOT:PSS is 0.5307 W·m−1·K−1, which is similar to the report by Yemata et al. [59]. It is found that the κ ⊥ of PEDOT:PSS increases with the addition of SG. In the case of the same content, κ ⊥ of PSG is higher than that of PS. GQDs have strong interaction with PEDOT:PSS [50]. Raman spectra in Fig. 4 also shows the GQDs enhance the interaction between PEDOT:PSS and SWCNTs, which weakens the phonon scattering and increases the heat transfer [60].

The κ ∥ of PEDOT:PSS is 1.490 W·m−1·K−1, close to the data reported by Liu et al. with 1.1 W·m−1·K−1 [61]. And a factor of κ ∥/κ ⊥ is equal to 2.8, close to the ideal factor. After adding SG and SWCNTs, κ ∥ of PEDOT:PSS increases obviously with the increase of the nanofillers content. Moreover, the κ ∥/κ ⊥ value is higher than 3 for the composite films, being attributed to highly ordered structure forming along in-plane direction and confirmed in the SEM images (Fig. 2). At the same content added, the κ ∥of PSG is improved obviously compared with PS. As above described, the strong interaction between nanotubes and PEDOT:PSS and among nanotubes brings good interfacial bonding via the modification of GQDs on the surface of SWCNTs, in turn, weakens the phonon scattering. According to ZT = S 2 σT/κ, the in-plane ZT value can be obtained as shown in Table 1. Compared to other samples, PSG1 has the highest ZT value and reaches 3.22 × 10−3 due to high PF value and low in-plane thermal conductivity. Therefore, the suitable modification of SWCNTs can effectively improve the thermoelectric properties of PEDOT:PSS.

In the present study, the addition of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to PEDOT:PSS/SWCNTs has improved the thermoelectric properties of the composite films. The strong interaction between the GQDs and PSS induced a separation of PSS from PEDOT. This separation enhanced the interaction between the SWCNTs and PEDOT and was beneficial for the formation of ordered alternate layered structures. In addition, the interfacial incorporation of GQDs improved the thermoelectric properties of the PEDOT:PSS/SWCNTs films. The suitable modification of SWCNTs can effectively improve the thermoelectric properties of PEDOT:PSS via energy-filtering effect. The results from the present study may benefit the research on organic thermoelectric composites and speed up the development of their applications.

Ping Fu: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis. Jin-Kun Xiao: Investigation, Methodology. Jia-Zhi Gong: Software, Validation. Ying Zhu: Validation, Formal analysis. Jun-An Yao: Formal analysis. Yun-Fei Zhang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Sheng-Gao Wang: Resources. Zhi-Dong Lin: Project administration, Resources. Fei-Peng Du: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51803157) and the Undergraduate Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program Project of the Hubei province (S202010490034).

Md. N. Hasan, H. Wahid, N. Nayan, M. S. M. Ali, Inorganic thermoelectric materials: A review, Int. J. Energy Res. 44 (2020) 6170‐6222.
